If the company wasn't overvalued, I would be sure to rate them as at least a HOLD, as they do have predictable growth, with decent dividends as well as manageable debt. Into the future, we will see if my thesis plays out, and if it does, I will be sure to revisit HD and update my rating to a possible better one.

data.title: Alpinum Investment Management Q2 2025 Letter
data.description:
“The Empire strikes back!”

In the US, President Donald Trump's January inauguration marked the start of a term with assertive trade policies. The administration implemented significant tariff hikes, including a 25% duty on Canadian steel and aluminium, a 25% tariff on European cars and a 30% levy on Chinese electronics. These moves intensified trade tensions, contributing to a 2.3% decline in the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) and raising (short-term) recession concerns. The Federal Reserve kept interest rates at 4.5%, balancing inflation control with signs of economic slowdown.

Chart 1: No recession in sight (so far)

Europe experienced a transformative geopolitical and economic shift, led by Germany’s landmark multi-hundred billion investment program, ending austerity. The euro surged to five-month highs, further supported by Ukraine’s acceptance of a proposed ceasefire, bolstering investor confidence. In China, authorities injected USD 200 billion into the financial sector to stabilize the property market and mitigate systemic risks. Despite these efforts, investor sentiment remained fragile, with the People’s Bank of China signalling further easing measures to support growth. The quarter underscored the fragility of the global economy, as trade disputes, policy uncertainties, and financial instability continue to weigh on growth prospects.

United States

In Q1 2025, the US economy exhibited notable resilience, buoyed by a robust labour market, with GDP growth maintaining a steady pace at 2.8% for 2024, marking the third consecutive year of expansion within the 2.5-3.0% range. Despite early signs of softening, the labour market remained a pivotal driver of consumption and crucial to economic growth. However, macroeconomic factors, including heightened policy uncertainty and escalating tariff risks, presented new challenges, particularly within manufacturing and trade sectors. The Trump administration’s imposition of a 25% tariff on steel and aluminium imports, with a potential 50% escalation for Canada, later deferred following negotiations, further strained trade relations. While tariffs on Canadian and Mexican goods were temporarily paused, the administration increased tariffs on Chinese imports to 20%, triggering retaliatory actions. The European Union also faced potential tariffs, signalling the intensification of trade conflicts.

Chart 2: S&P 500 companies citing “tariffs” on earnings calls

Concurrently, the S&P 500 exhibited considerable volatility, driven by the surge in DeepSeek's valuation in January, which spurred a recalibration of AI and tech stocks. Simultaneously, the aggressive tariff policy, combined with rising trade tensions, amplified market uncertainty. This led to a 2.3% decline in the index in the first quarter of the year as investors reassessed growth prospects amid escalating geopolitical risks and inflationary pressures. The US inflation remained persistent, with the CPI decelerating to 2.8% in February from 3.0% in January. In response to persistent inflation, particularly in non-housing services, the Federal Reserve adopted a cautious stance, signalling a“higher for longer” monetary policy, with two rate cuts anticipated in 2025, while maintaining the long-term policy rate in the mid-4% range.

Europe

The European economy expanded at a subdued pace, navigating a complex landscape of monetary stimulus, fiscal interventions and intensifying geopolitical tensions. The European Central Bank (ECB) executed a 25-basis-point reduction in its benchmark rate to 2.5%, marking the sixth consecutive cut within a year. This sustained monetary accommodation sought to counteract lacklustre economic activity, prompting the ECB to revise its 2025 GDP growth forecast downwards to 0.9% from 1.1%, while inflation was projected to average 2.3%. The labour market remained resilient, though wage growth moderated amid persistent economic uncertainties. Fiscal policy diverged across the region, with Germany unveiling a landmark multi-hundred billion investment program aimed at revitalizing its domestic economy, signaling a shift away from austerity, while potentially exerting broader inflationary and growth implications across the eurozone.

Chart 3: European equities outperforming in 2025

The political landscape was reshaped by the German federal election on 23 February, where the CDU/CSU secured 28.5% of the vote, emerging as the largest parliamentary bloc. The far-right AfD achieved an unprecedented 20.8%, underscoring a shift in voter sentiment and complicating coalition negotiations, with ramifications for both domestic and EU policy trajectories. Geopolitical tensions escalated as the United States imposed a 25% tariff on European steel and aluminium, prompting the EU to prepare retaliatory levies on USD 28 billion of US goods, effective April, amplifying trade war concerns. The US dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) weakened to a five-month low. Equity markets staged a robust recovery, with the STOXX Europe 600 advancing 8.3% year-to-date, outpacing the S&P 500. European equities, trading at a forward P/E of 14x versus 22x for US stocks, benefited from favourable valuations and ECB support.

China and emerging markets ('EM')

China’s economic trajectory in Q1 2025 was shaped by a delicate balance between policy recalibration, persistent disinflationary trends, and heightened geopolitical risks. While GDP surpassed 130 trillion yuan (USD 17.82 trillion) for the first time, growing at 5% in line with official targets, underlying momentum remained fragile. The National Bureau of Statistics reported contracting manufacturing activity and subdued consumer sentiment, with January PMIs disappointing and servicesector expansion losing steam. External headwinds intensified as escalating trade frictions with the US and seasonal distortions, including the Lunar New Year, contributed to an unexpected decline in imports and weakening export momentum. The People's Bank of China(PBoC) maintained a cautious monetary stance, prioritizing currency stability amid ongoing depreciation pressures, while inflation remained volatile, with a transient 0.5% rise in January followed by a sharp -0.7% contraction in February, reinforcing concerns over entrenched disinflation.

Chart 4: 1-year performance Hang Seng Tech vs Nasdaq 100

Equities rebounded on state intervention, led by a 24.7% YTD surge in the Hang Seng Tech Index, fuelled by President Xi’s corporate outreach and speculation over regulatory easing. Offshore equities remained vulnerable to geopolitical risks, while onshore markets displayed relative resilience. The PBoC’s suspension of bond purchases fuelled short-end volatility, flattening the yield curve. Geopolitical uncertainty loomed large, with the Trump administration’s tariff threats amplifying risks to China’s export-driven growth model. The yuan showed relative resilience, depreciating 0.5% YTD. Structural fragilities in the property sector, tepid private investment and constrained credit transmission mechanisms weighed on sentiment, reinforcing expectations that fiscal stimulus alone would prove insufficient to engineer a durable recovery.

Investment conclusions

The global economic landscape is undergoing a profound transformation, characterized by the dismantling of old structures and a realignment of power. Higher tariffs and increasing economic isolation are pushing up prices, resulting in long-term productivity losses and subdued growth prospects. Significant investments in defence and infrastructure provide a much-needed boost to stagnating regions like Europe, though this also creates delayed inflationary pressures. In response, markets are adjusting to higher risk premiums as rising debt levels compound the challenges. The US faces low growth, while the EU tries to break free from stagnation and China pursues its ambitious 5% growth target. Structural inflation, driven by de-globalization, tariffs and energy transition, intersects with geopolitical tensions and tighter financial conditions. Despite these challenges, large-cap technology stocks and the US housing market show resilience, while risk aversion permeates the broader market.

Chart 5: Yield-to-worst of global bond segments

Bonds: Fixed income offers compelling total return opportunities amid widening credit spreads and a steeper European yield curve. We favour short-duration high-yield and loans, while duration remains an effective portfolio diversifier.

Equities: Fiscal stimulus in Europe and China, alongside USD weakness, bolsters ex-US equities short-term. We maintain a positive equity bias with a blended style approach, acknowledging persistent volatility and limited upside in large-cap US equities.

The tactical approach emphasizes balance, with a slight preference for value and cyclicals amid resilient growth. Duration is neutral, maintaining a balanced view on IG bonds and USTs, while favouring short-term HY, loans and selective below-IG bonds and hybrids.

data.title: Domino's Pizza: A Slice Of Profitable Growth - Why Investors Should Take A Bite
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Abhishek Gajula as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Domino's Pizza (NASDAQ:DPZ) has showcased robust growth through its unique technology driven business approach and widespread network of franchises, achieving tremendous market share in the Quick Service Restaurant sector. DPZ's forward price to earnings (P/E) Ratio of nearly 27x and enterprise value to EBITDA of about 22x suggests that the stock is fairly priced in coordination with projected future growth. DPZ is expected to grow at about 7% annually for the next 10 years. (which i will be expanding in the growth estimates section). I rate Dominos as a definite BUY.

Introduction

Domino's Pizza is almost unanimously agreed upon to be a market leader in the global Pizza industry. With a franchise network spanning more than 20,000 stores across more than 90 different countries, Domino's has made for itself one of the largest business networks globally. It's unique market approach focusing mainly on digitality has contributed to about 75% sales (through online medium) in the U.S. This strategic market approach is a key factor in DPZ's dominating historical performance. While Investors may be concerned about a slight deceleration in growth, such as the Q4 Global retail sales falling to 4.4% compared to the previous years 6%, inflationary challenges and increased labor expenses, DPZ's operational income still increased by 6.5% in Q4, highlighting their consistent performance despite short term hurdles. Despite temporary headwinds, DPZ's valuation still appears to remain strong, considering factors such as the attractive growth potential globally and their ability to perform consistently mainly through their unique digital approach to the market. With strategic growth based reinvestments and innovative delivery models, DPZ is a stock well positioned to lock in its stance as a market leader and generate long term shareholder value.

Growth Estimates

I expect a 7% overall annual growth rate for Domino's Pizza in the years to come, driven majorly by balanced franchise expansion coupled with Same-store sales (SSS) increase. This estimate assumes an overall store count growth of about 4% annually, paired with a SSS growth of about 3%. Historical performance supports this assumption: Domino's revenue rose from $2.42 billion in 2015 to $4.71 Billion in 2024, achieving a CAGR of 7.2%. In 2024 alone, Domino's added about 775 new stores across the world, totaling up to 21,366 stores worldwide, which is a tremendous 3.76% increase while global retail sales grew by about 4.64%, with U.S Same store sales standing at 3.2% and international same store sales at 1.6%. I’ve taken 3% same store sales growth rate as it is in-line with the Global economic growth rate. Also Dominos has enjoyed a 3.8% increase in the number of new stores over the past year, highlighting comfortable growth over recent years. Here is a look at DPZ’s store count growth over the years:

Historically Dominos has achieved a fascinating growth rate of about 5% for their store count across the globe (Refer Graph Above). However taking a conservative stance, I assume the store count growth to hover around 4% annually, inline with growth projections for global economy. This growth would help Dominos reach their overall store count to about 31,000 by 2032. Something interesting I've observed here: DPZ has opened more stores internationally over the last year than domestically, showcasing strong commitment and potential for global expansion. I've assumed a 4% overall growth rate for DPZ mainly driven by their aggressive store count expansion. Post 2032, Assuming that the store count stays constant (from a conservative viewpoint) , We can estimate the same store sales to grow by about 4% which falls inline with the global economic growth projections. Therefore I estimate a terminal growth rate of about 4%.

Is Domino's Pizza's Debt a risk?

Dominos has a debt of about $3.83 Billion (As of December 2024). This debt was majorly taken to further boost expansion and grow their store network. The Debt to EBITDA ratio stands at about 3.79x, the ratio being slightly on the higher end. This means the company needs about 3.7 years of consistent and stable performance to repay all it's debts.

Domino's Pizza ability to manage their Debt's efficiently is what makes it an attractive opportunity to me. The reported interest coverage ratio for the company stood at 4.5x (As of December 31, 2024). This particular ratio showcases the ability of Dominos to deliver stability on a consistent basis, managing their interest payments without compromising their shareholder returns. Adding on, the company's total debt has remained stable. These figures suggest that Domino's maintains a well-thought approach to debt management, ensuring its financial obligations are well-taken care of. This debt has so far managed to be positive to shareholders as it boosted the overall growth and expansion of the company.

DCF valuation

As discussed earlier I've assumed a 7% growth rate (during the projected period until 2032) followed by a 4% terminal growth rate for future growth of DPZ, highlighting the future expansion that I am assuming for Dominos over these 7 years. This growth is mainly driven by DPZ's rapid store count growth coupled with ever strengthening brand identity. I am assuming 9% discount rate as it is inline with earlier market growth rate.

The valuation model I've taken gives us a resulting share value of about $447, signaling that the stock is fairly valued. This indicates that the stock is fairly priced for a 9% return i.e The Dominos Shareholder can comfortably expect a 9% return annually, assuming that the company's performance is inline with projected growth rate. Temporary market fluctuations could impact DPZ's returns, yet the strong branding of Dominos coupled with rapid store count increase and consistent innovation in marketing strategies would eventually stabilize the situation putting dominos back on track in terms of stable returns. However here are a few risks I would like to discuss upon when it comes to a stock like Dominos.

Risks Involved for Potential Shareholders

Excess Dependence on store count growth

Domino's Pizza as of my observation depends heavily upon physical store count growth, mainly in international markets, for its overall expansion. I've assumed a 4% annual increase in store counts to support the overall 7% growth rate. However, the assumption I've taken comes with a few risks. Expanding Internationally through physical outlets involves complex tasks such as regulation problems, cultural & economic differences, economic instabilities which stand as an obstacle for healthy growth even for a company as DPZ. U.S being a mature market, limits growth opportunities, automatically making international expansion seem like a better option for most companies. Any failure to execute this expansion effectively, could take a solid hit on Domino's growth leading to a potential down which investors do not prefer.

Debt Burden

I've discussed earlier that DPZ carries a debt of $3.83 Billion (as of December 2024) with a debt to EBITDA ratio of about 3.79. Although the reported interest coverage ratio is 4.5, from which I would infer that DPZ can handle interest payments efficiently, There is always a possibility for short term market fluctuation, making it a minor risk for Investors. I'd say minor because for a company like domino's which relies heavily upon its well established market model, such temporary fluctuations leave behind a minimal effect, But the risk still persists.

Margin Pressure

Dominos recently has been facing increased pressure on their margins pertaining to operations, mainly because of labor cost increase. As I've mentioned earlier Although there were Higher sales in 2024, the Operating income in Q4 2024 grew by only a 6.5% signaling the fact that cost increases are more as compared to revenue gains. Factors such as Inflation of Labor, Raw materials and disruptions in supply chains could further reduce overall returns. This further reduces possibilities for reinvestments in growth initiatives, challenging DPZ to maintain its stock value.

Who is this a right fit for?

Growth Oriented Investors with Moderate Risk Tolerance

In-line with the growth estimate I've taken earlier. I Project DPZ to grow at an overall growth rate of 7% with a 4% terminal growth rate combined by 3% same store sales growth. With Forward P/E of 27x and EV/EBITDA of about 22x, DPZ to me is fairly valued. Domino's is a must buy to investors seeking growth at a comfortable and a reasonable price making it probably one of the best available options.

Long Term / Value based investors

A valuation of about $447 per share indirectly signals the 9% annual return rate I am assuming for DPZ, making it a fruitful bet for Investors who are willing to look at a 5-10 year game. DPZ's Expansionary strategies combined with a conservative stance (As discussed earlier), with projection to reach 31,000 stores worldwide by 2032 highlight DPZ's long term growth potential perfect for value based investors making DPZ a perfect fit for their Portfolio.

Defensive Investors

DPZ is a company that has a reliable market stance irrespective of temporary headwinds and market fluctuations. Defensive Investors who tend to value stable returns and resilient market stance will find Dominos as a fruitful and promising bet, mainly due to their projected overall growth rate which in my assumption is 7%. Coupled with my 9% annual return rate, DPZ is projected to deliver consistent performance while delivering the extra layer of protection Defensive investor's look for.

Conclusion

Dominos stands tall as a promising buy for all investors looking at a stock that ensures long term value based growth. Being a market leader in the Quick Service Restaurant Sector, Domino's is a brand that is backed by its fruitful franchise growth across continents and digitality based market approach. I project Domino's to possess a global network of about 31,000 stores by the year 2032. This growth assumption alone, even from a conservative stance, is a testament to DPZ's future growth potential ensuring consistent returns, Which makes it an absolutely attractive buy for me. Although there are temporary fluctuations and growth obstacles, DPZ is one such company that can manage these situations effectively. To me, Dominos is an opportunity that promises consistency coupled with stability. This isn't just any other stock, I consider it a slice of tomorrow's profits.
